summary: DHH argues that while the S&P 500 has recovered, the gains are concentrated in profitable mega-cap tech companies like Apple and Microsoft, leaving unprofitable SaaS companies still deeply underwater. He contends that the era of cheap capital lifting all tech boats is over, and actual business fundamentals now determine valuations. Companies like Asana, Monday, and Smartsheet are burning through hundreds of millions annually, while private companies like ClickUp, Airtable, and Notion face brutal fundraising conditions after raising at peak valuations. DHH warns that while large-scale money-losing companies can survive for years on accumulated capital, the financial bleeding will eventually prove fatal.
key_points: The 2023 market recovery is concentrated in a handful of profitable mega-cap tech stocks, not a broad tech rebound
Unprofitable SaaS companies that raised at peak 2021 valuations face devastating down-rounds as they exhaust their runway
Public SaaS companies like Asana ($400M annual loss) and Smartsheet ($230M annual loss) have unsustainable burn rates now that capital is no longer free
Large-scale money-losing companies can survive for years on accumulated capital — Groupon is still alive 12 years after IPO despite collapsing from $25B to $200M
The shift back to fundamentals-based valuation means more pain is coming for unprofitable enterprise SaaS
money_quotes: We're now back in world where the actual business metrics and mechanics of individual operations determine the value assigned to their stocks and startups.
When fresh capital is inevitable needed, it'll be murder on the cap tables.
It's hard to know whether or how that'll all come to an end, but those aren't pretty numbers now that capital is no longer free.
At a large enough scale, there's substantial lag between the infliction of a mortal financial wound and the final burial of the bleeding company.
You'd be silly, as an investor, employee, or customer, not to pay attention to the red drain once it's begun.
It's a truism that if you bleed long enough, you will eventually die, though, and boy has there been a lot of bleeding already!
key_insight: The return to fundamentals-based valuation means unprofitable SaaS companies face an existential reckoning — the bubble era subsidized their losses, and no recovery is coming to bail them out.
